summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Larry Gonick's mathematical cartoon series originally published in Discover magazine, covering topics from neural nets to cryptography to game theory. He catalogs Gonick's extensive bibliography of cartoon guides spanning history, science, and technical subjects. Atwood distinguishes between shallow quick-learning gimmicks (like 'Learn X in 24 Hours' books) and genuinely effective visual learning through well-crafted cartoons. He endorses cartoon-based learning as a legitimate and effective educational approach for complex topics.
key_points: Larry Gonick has created an extensive catalog of cartoon-based educational content covering mathematics, science, history, and technical topics
Visual and cartoon-based learning can make complex subjects like cryptography, neural nets, and game theory more accessible
There's a meaningful difference between gimmicky 'learn fast' approaches and genuinely engaging educational formats
Fun and engagement are important factors in effective learning
money_quotes: Although I made fun of the Learn ASP.NET in 24 Hours meme, I am a big fan of learning subjects in cartoon form.
You learn more when you're having fun doing it.
He's famous for his easy to grasp, beautifully illustrated treatment of complex topics.
key_insight: Cartoon-based learning isn't a shortcut or gimmick—it's a legitimately effective way to understand complex topics because engagement and enjoyment enhance retention.
